articles.subject: Endocrinology and metabolic disorders
articles.title: Diabetes mellitus

sections.heading: Summary
sections.body: Diabetes mellitus is chronic hyperglycemia from absolute (type 1) or relative (type 2) insulin deficiency. Type 1 is autoimmune β-cell destruction; type 2 is insulin resistance with progressive β-cell failure, usually linked to obesity. Diagnose with glucose or HbA1c criteria; treat glycemia while screening for and preventing micro- and macrovascular complications.

sections.heading: Type 1 vs type 2
sections.body: T1DM accounts for ~5–10% of cases and often presents in childhood with acute symptoms or DKA. T2DM is far more common, frequently undiagnosed for years, and strongly associated with obesity and family history.
bullets: T1DM: HLA-DR3/DR4, autoimmune β-cell loss, low/absent C-peptide, high DKA risk → insulin always
T2DM: insulin resistance + β-cell dysfunction, obesity-linked, low DKA risk → lifestyle, metformin, then add-ons
Classic triad: polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia (± weight loss) — more prominent in T1DM
T2DM clues: acanthosis nigricans, skin tags, recurrent infections, often asymptomatic at diagnosis
Other types to know: gestational DM, LADA (late autoimmune T1), MODY (autosomal dominant, onset <25 y)

citations.title: About diabetes
citations.author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.body: Insulin is the only hormone that directly lowers blood glucose. Proinsulin cleavage produces insulin and C-peptide — a useful marker when type is unclear.
bullets: T1DM: environmental trigger in genetically susceptible host → autoantibodies (anti-GAD, anti-ICA) → β-cell destruction → absolute insulin deficiency
T2DM: central obesity → ↑ free fatty acids → impaired insulin signaling (IRS-1, GLUT4) → insulin resistance; amylin deposits worsen β-cell function over time
Early T2DM: compensatory hyperinsulinemia; later: postprandial then fasting hyperglycemia as secretion fails

citations.title: Insulin and glucagon: partners for life

sections.heading: Presentation & screening
sections.body: T1DM usually presents abruptly; [[Diabetic ketoacidosis]] is the first manifestation in up to half of cases. T2DM develops insidiously and may present only when complications appear or during routine labs.
bullets: Screen all adults ≥35 years; screen younger patients if overweight/obese plus ≥1 risk factor (FHx, HTN, dyslipidemia, PCOS, GDM history, inactivity, high-risk ethnicity)
Repeat normal screens every 3 years; annually if prediabetes; every 1–3 years after gestational diabetes
Suspect DM with unexplained weight loss, blurred vision, fatigue, poor wound healing, or recurrent UTIs/candidiasis

citations.title: Screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes
citations.author: USPSTF

citations.title: About diabetes
citations.author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.body: Confirm diabetes with symptoms plus random glucose ≥200 mg/dL, or with two abnormal tests on separate days in asymptomatic patients.
bullets: Initial workup: BMP, UACR, CBC, LFTs, lipid panel
Type unclear? Check C-peptide (low in T1DM) and islet autoantibodies (anti-GAD first; then IA-2, ZnT8)
[!warning] HbA1c can be unreliable with hemoglobinopathies, anemia, CKD, or pregnancy — use glucose testing when discordant
Diabetes: FPG ≥126, 2-h OGTT ≥200, or HbA1c ≥6.5%
Prediabetes: FPG 100–125, OGTT 140–199, or HbA1c 5.7–6.4%

sections.heading: Management principles
sections.body: Goals are symptom relief, individualized glycemic targets, and comprehensive prevention of complications. Avoid hypoglycemia — it limits how aggressively you can treat.
bullets: Typical adult targets: HbA1c <7%, preprandial glucose 80–130 mg/dL, postprandial <180 mg/dL (individualize)
Lifestyle: ≥150 min/week moderate activity, resistance training 2–3×/week, weight loss ≥3–7% in T2DM with obesity
ASCVD prevention: BP control, statin for age ≥40, ACEi/ARB if albuminuria, antiplatelet therapy when indicated
Check HbA1c every 3–6 months; screen microvascular complications at T2DM diagnosis or 5 years after T1DM onset, then yearly

callouts.body: Avoid hypoglycemia — it limits how aggressively you can push glycemic targets, especially in elderly or CKD patients. Individualize: a frail 80-year-old does not need an HbA1c <7%.

sections.heading: Pharmacotherapy
sections.body: T1DM requires insulin from diagnosis. T2DM starts with lifestyle plus pharmacotherapy at diagnosis — [[Metformin]] first for most patients without compelling CV or kidney indications for another agent.
bullets: T1DM: MDI or pump; typical TDD 0.4–1.0 units/kg/day (roughly 50% basal / 50% bolus)
T2DM first-line: [[Metformin]]; add SGLT2i and/or GLP-1 RA if ASCVD, high ASCVD risk, HF, or CKD
Step up therapy sequentially; consider insulin if HbA1c >10%, glucose ≥300, catabolic symptoms, or targets unmet on oral agents
Prefer GLP-1 RA before insulin when appropriate; do not combine DPP-4 inhibitors with GLP-1 RAs
Acute crises: [[Diabetic ketoacidosis]] (T1DM >> T2DM) and [[Hyperglycemic crisis]] (often elderly T2DM)

callouts.body: Agents that help weight & CV/renal outcomes — “the good G's”: GLP-1 RAs and the “gliflozins” (SGLT2i). Reach for these in ASCVD, HF, or CKD.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.body: Acute: DKA, hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS), and treatment-related hypoglycemia. Chronic hyperglycemia drives microvascular injury (nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy, foot disease) and macrovascular atherosclerosis (MI, stroke, PAD) — the leading causes of morbidity and death.
bullets: Annual screening: UACR + eGFR, dilated eye exam, monofilament foot exam
Strict glycemic control best prevents microvascular disease; also manage BP and lipids aggressively
Prediabetes: ~10% annual progression to T2DM — lifestyle (≥7% weight loss) first; consider metformin in high-risk adults
Pediatrics: >90% T1DM; screen T2DM in obese children ≥10 y with risk factors; sulfonylureas, TZDs, DPP-4i, and SGLT2i have limited pediatric approval

articles.highYield: T1DM = autoimmune β-cell destruction (HLA-DR3/DR4), absolute insulin deficiency, high DKA risk — always insulin. T2DM = insulin resistance + β-cell failure, obesity-linked — metformin first; add SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for ASCVD, HF, or CKD. Diagnose: FPG ≥126, OGTT ≥200, HbA1c ≥6.5%, or random ≥200 + symptoms.
articles.summary: Diabetes mellitus is chronic hyperglycemia from absolute (type 1) or relative (type 2) insulin deficiency. T1DM is autoimmune and insulin-dependent; T2DM is insulin resistance with progressive β-cell failure. Diagnose with standard glucose/HbA1c thresholds, treat glycemia while preventing micro- and macrovascular complications, and screen complications annually.

questions.subject: Endocrinology
questions.text: A 14-year-old presents with polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss. Labs show glucose 420 mg/dL and positive anti-GAD antibodies. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Anti-GAD antibodies and acute classic symptoms in an adolescent support autoimmune T1DM.

questions.subject: Endocrinology
questions.text: Which finding best distinguishes prediabetes from diabetes by HbA1c?

questions.subject: Endocrinology
questions.text: A patient with T2DM, eGFR 45, and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Which drug class is preferred?
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors are preferred in T2DM with HF and/or CKD.

questions.subject: Endocrinology
questions.text: A thin 8-year-old presents in DKA. Which feature most strongly supports type 1 over type 2 diabetes?
options: Family history of T2DM in a parent
questions.explanation: Autoantibodies confirm autoimmune T1DM; obesity and acanthosis nigricans favor T2DM.

flashcards.back: FPG ≥126, 2-h OGTT ≥200, HbA1c ≥6.5%, or random ≥200 + symptoms

flashcards.front: First-line pharmacologic therapy for most T2DM?
flashcards.back: Metformin (unless contraindicated or CV/CKD indication for alternative)

flashcards.front: Preferred agents in T2DM with ASCVD, HF, or CKD?
flashcards.back: SGLT2 inhibitors and/or GLP-1 receptor agonists

flashcards.front: When to start microvascular complication screening?
flashcards.back: At T2DM diagnosis; 5 years after T1DM onset — then annually

articles.subject: Endocrinology and metabolic disorders
articles.title: Diabetic ketoacidosis

sections.heading: Summary
sections.body: [[Diabetic ketoacidosis]] (DKA) is an acute metabolic emergency defined by hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. It most often occurs in [[Diabetes mellitus]] type 1 but can present in type 2 under severe stress.

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.body: Look for polyuria, polydipsia, abdominal pain, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status.
bullets: Glucose usually >250 mg/dL with pH <7.3 and bicarbonate <18 mEq/L
Positive serum/urine ketones with anion gap acidosis
Search for precipitant: infection, missed insulin, MI, new-onset diabetes

callouts.body: Give potassium before/with insulin once K⁺ <5.2 mEq/L and urine output is adequate — insulin drives K⁺ intracellularly and can precipitate fatal hypokalemia. Hold insulin if K⁺ <3.3 mEq/L.

articles.highYield: Fluid resuscitation + IV insulin + potassium replacement. Never stop insulin until anion gap closes.

sections.heading: Summary
sections.body: [[Metformin]] is the first-line oral agent for type 2 [[Diabetes mellitus]]. It lowers hepatic glucose output and improves peripheral insulin sensitivity with a low hypoglycemia risk.

sections.heading: Safety
sections.body: Hold before iodinated contrast and major surgery if eGFR is borderline. The main rare serious toxicity is lactic acidosis in renal failure.

callouts.body: Contraindicated when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m². Hold at the time of iodinated contrast in at-risk patients and around major surgery; restart once renal function is confirmed stable.
Metformin is weight-neutral to weight-favorable and does not cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy — a key reason it stays first-line.
